---
title: "Discussion"
output: pdf_document
bibliography: references.bib
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: sentence
---

A persistent challenge in meta-analysis is the prevalence of dependent data structures, such as arise when primary studies report results for multiple outcomes or multiple samples. 
Methods for summary meta-analysis of dependent effect sizes are now well-developed, but there are fewer available methods for investigating selective reporting bias with dependent data structures.
Approximate adjustment methods based on Egger-style regression have recently been adapted to accommodate dependent effects [@chen2024adapting], but more principled approaches such as p-value selection models were, until recently, limited to meta-analyses with independent effects. 
In this work, we developed and evaluated a marginal beta-density selection model, extending a model proposed by @citkowicz2017parsimonious for meta-analyses with independent effects. To accommodate dependent effects, we followed a strategy established in our previous work on the step-function selection model [@pustejovsky2025step], in which we focus on modeling the marginal distribution of effect size estimates and handle dependence using CRVE or clustered bootstrapping. 

Modeling the _marginal_ distribution of effect sizes amounts to treating all effects as if they were independent observations for purposes of parameter estimation. This strategy is pragmatic because it avoids the need for complex assumptions about the joint selection process of a set of effects generated in a given study and sidesteps the high-dimensional integration that would be required for multivariate selection models. Pairing this "working independence" assumption with cluster-robust variance estimation or clustered bootstrapping provides a feasible strategy that accounts for the dependence structure of the data during the inference stage. Under this framework, the marginal model provides the point estimates but deliberately ignores the dependence structure, while CRVE or bootstrapping provides approximate assessments of uncertainty that adjust for within-study dependence.

Our earlier work demonstrated that this framework works well with marginal step-function selection models [@pustejovsky2025step].  
By incorporating the beta-density selection function [@citkowicz2017parsimonious] into the marginal modeling framework, we provide a distinct and complementary representation of the selection process. 
The step-function selection model assumes that selection probabilities are piecewise-constant, only shifting at specific, pre-specified threshold $p$-values. 
The beta-density selection model offers a flexible alternative for capturing smoothly varying selection probabilities, without requiring meta-analysts to identify specific $p$-value thresholds a priori. 
Using only two selection parameters, this approach allows for a parsimonious representation of subtle, non-linear reporting patterns that would be difficult to approximate by a fixed-threshold step function model.

Our simulations extend prior work in several ways. First, we considered how well the marginal beta-density model works when the data-generating process involves dependent effect size estimates that are selectively reported following a process that is compatible with the assumptions of the beta-density model---scenarios that are relatively favorable for the proposed approach.
Across all simulation conditions examined, the beta-density selection model demonstrated negligible or small bias and consistently outperformed CHE-ISCW and PET/PEESE in capturing the true average effect. 
However, results regarding RMSE revealed a trade-off between bias reduction and model complexity. 
The beta-density model achieved the lowest RMSE under conditions of strong or extreme selection (specifically, when $\mu = 0$, or when $\mu = 0.2$ and heterogeneity was moderate to large), but the CHE-ISCW estimator was more accurate under conditions where selective reporting did not lead to a high degree of censoring (i.e., either because selective reporting was absent or because primary studies had high power). 
This trade-off arises because the added variability introduced by estimating the beta-density selection model outweighs its minimal bias reduction under conditions with weak selective reporting, a pattern that is consistent with prior simulations of the marginal step function model [@chen2024adapting; @pustejovsky2025step]. 
Regarding inference, we found that large-sample CRVE confidence intervals for all methods had coverage below the nominal 95% rate---particularly for CHE-ISCW and PET/PEESE because they centered on biased estimates. 
In contrast, two-stage percentile bootstrap confidence intervals for the beta-density model parameters worked much better, consistently providing the most reliable coverage levels.
<!-- JEP: Add comments about acceptable J to trust the CIs? -->

The second part of our simulations examined the robustness of the previously developed marginal step-function selection model to functional misspecification.
In particular, we examined whether single-step or two-step marginal selection models could provide an adequate approximation to a more complex form of selection process based on the beta density model.
Because the data were generated via the smooth beta density process, the beta model estimate of average effect was naturally less biased than the estimates based on step-function models. 
The one-step model produced biased estimates and miscalibrated, overly narrow confidence intervals under many conditions.
The two-step model was less biased, suggesting that its more flexible specification allowed for a closer approximation to the true selection process, but still did not provide adequately calibrated confidence intervals. 
However, the estimates of average effect from the mis-specfied step-function models were sometimes more accurate (with lower RMSE) than estimates from the beta density model, particularly under conditions with milder selective reporting.
in conditions where the true average effect was moderate or large and selection was not extreme, even the simpler one-step model had lower RMSE than the beta-density model. 
Even though the beta density model has the same number of parameters as the two-step model, its parameters appear to be more difficult to estimate except under strong or extreme levels of selection.
This suggests that the beta density model may be most useful for larger meta-analyses, with more information available for estimating the functional form of the selection process.

## Limitations and Future Directions

A primary limitation of the current study is that we have examined robustness to only one possible form of misspecification. 
Because our simulations used the beta density as the true selection mechanism, the conditions were naturally favorable to estimates based on the correctly specified beta model and relatively less favorable to the mis-specified step-function selection models. 
A critical next step is to evaluate the "reverse" scenario: assessing the performance of the beta-density selection model when the true selection process is characterized by discrete, threshold-based jumps. 
Such an investigation would clarify whether the beta curve is flexible enough to approximate sharp discontinuities without introducing substantial bias.

<!-- Add limitation regarding prior specification, unit information priors, potential to develop empirically informed priors for selection parameters. -->

Furthermore, our focus on the marginal distribution of effect sizes is most readily compatible with selection processes that operate primarily at the level of individual outcomes, or what is termed "selective outcome reporting." Although our "working independence" approach using PML and RVE or bootstrapping handles the resulting data dependencies effectively, it does not explicitly model study-level selection (i.e., publication bias), in which an entire cluster of effects might be suppressed based on the significance of a "headline" finding or pattern of several findings rather than on the individual $p$-values of each outcome. 

Contemporaneously with our work, several other novel models have been proposed for selective reporting with dependent effect sizes. 
@VanAert2026correcting introduced novel selection models for study-level publication bias in meta-analyses involving multivariate or multilevel outcomes. The central assumption of their approach is that the probability of publication depends on whether all or none of the specific findings within a study are statistically significant.
As part of an ensemble of Bayesian models, @Bartos2026robust proposed a step-function selection model for meta-analysis data that have a multilevel structure;
their approach assumes that selective outcome reporting occurs at the level of individual effect sizes and that selection decisions are mutually independent conditional on the true average effect for each study. 
In contrast to our penalized marginal likelihood approach, @Bartos2026robust estimate the model using the full posterior distribution of the parameters, and so fitting the model is computationally intensive. 
It would be useful to conduct head-to-head comparisons between their conditional step-function model and our marginal modeling approach in order to better understand the benefits and drawbacks of using the full posterior versus only the marginal likelihood.
Considering this variety of recently developed models, there is also an outstanding need to develop tools for model fit assessment and model comparison.
The recently proposed $Z$-curve plot [@Bartos2025zcurve] might prove to be useful for assessing specification of the selection process in these models.

Finally, a notable feature of selection models is that they can be extended to incorporate predictors (moderators) that explain heterogeneity in the average effect. 
In the present study, we focused exclusively on the estimation of the marginal average effect size and did not include predictors in our simulation design. 
Although we have developed the beta-density selection model to allow for meta-regression coefficients, we have not yet tested its performance in settings where effect sizes are moderated by covariates. 
Examining the accuracy and power of marginal selection models to detect moderator effects remains an important avenue for future research.
It is also possible in principle to allow the degree of heterogeneity to vary depending on predictors, as in location-scale meta-regression models [@viechtbauer2022locationscale], or even to allow the parameters of the selection process to vary depending on features of the study, such as whether it was pre-registered [cf. @vanaert2025metaanalyzing; @coburn2015publication].

## Conclusions

The beta-density selection model, estimated using a PML approach based on a composite marginal likelihood, offers a flexible alternative to step-function models or regression adjustment methods for selective reporting in meta-analyses with dependent data. 
By leveraging a marginal modeling strategy, it provides a bridge between the restrictive assumptions of the step-function selection model and the computational complexity of multivariate selection models. 
Although our results highlight a bias–variance trade-off—--where the beta model excels in high-selection contexts but may be less precise than simpler models when selection is absent—--the correctly specified beta-density model remained the most robust choice for maintaining 95% interval coverage of average effect sizes when paired with the two-stage percentile bootstrap.

Given its performance in reducing bias, the beta-density selection model is a promising tool both for sensitivity analysis and for estimation, particularly in larger meta-analytic databases. 
However, because the true selection process is unknown in practice and potentially varies across research fields and contexts, any choice of model requires assumptions about the reporting process. 
We understand the beta-density selection model not as representing the "true" mechanism, but rather as a flexible functional form that can approximate a wide range of smoothly varying selection patterns. 
Pending further research into its performance under threshold-based selection, study-level publication selection, and meta-regression contexts, we recommend adding the beta-density selection model to the expanding toolkit of complementary models for examining selective reporting.
